summary: Factoring rewrites a sum as a product — pull out common factors first, then recognize difference-of-squares and trinomial patterns.
explanation: Always check for a greatest common factor (GCF) first: factor it out of every term before anything else. A difference of two perfect squares always factors as \((a-b)(a+b)\) — this pattern shows up constantly and is easy to miss when the terms aren't obviously square. For a trinomial \(x^2 + bx + c\), look for two numbers that multiply to \(c\) and add to \(b\); those numbers become the constants in the two binomial factors. A perfect square trinomial (\(a^2+2ab+b^2\) or \(a^2-2ab+b^2\)) factors as a single squared binomial. Factoring is most often used to solve equations (set each factor equal to zero) or to simplify fractions (cancel common factors in the numerator and denominator).
